extra.message: SQL Injection in Dashboard Search - unsanitized user input directly interpolated into SQL query using f-strings
BOLA - Unauthenticated API Profile Access (IDOR). The @token_required decorator is commented out, allowing anyone to retrieve any user's profile information without authentication.
BFLA - Unauthenticated User Deletion. The @token_required decorator is commented out on the DELETE endpoint, allowing unauthenticated attackers to delete any user account.
Mass Assignment / BOPA - Privilege Escalation. The update_profile endpoint accepts role and permissions fields from POST form body, allowing any user to escalate their privileges to admin.
Hardcoded SECRET_KEY in configuration file
Path Traversal in File Upload - arbitrary filenames without validation allowing directory traversal
Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) in Profile Picture Update - user-supplied URL fetched without validation
Weak Password Hashing (MD5 Without Salt). The application uses MD5 to hash passwords without any salt, which is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password storage.
Missing Rate Limiting on Authentication Endpoints. The login endpoint has no rate limiting, account lockout, or delay mechanisms, allowing brute-force attacks and credential stuffing.
Unauthenticated Profile Picture Update via API
Stored XSS via Profile Country Field with Jinja2 `| safe` Filter Bypass
Reflected XSS via Search Parameter with Jinja2 `| safe` Filter Bypass
Weak MD5 password hashing without salt
Weak MD5 password hashing without salt
Session Cookie HttpOnly flag explicitly disabled, allowing XSS-based session hijacking
Session Cookie Secure flag not configured, allowing MITM attacks
Session Cookie SameSite attribute not configured, enabling CSRF attacks
Unrestricted file upload allows any file type without validation
Path traversal vulnerability in file deletion endpoint
Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) in profile picture update endpoint

Missing Authentication on User Profile Endpoint (GET /api/v1/profile)

The GET /api/v1/profile endpoint has the @token_required decorator commented out (lines 152-153) and accepts unauthenticated requests. Any anonymous user can retrieve any user's profile information (username, email, country, role, permissions, team) by providing a user_id query parameter. This exposes PII without any authentication.

Impact: An unauthenticated attacker can enumerate all user profiles and extract PII including email addresses, roles, permissions, and team assignments for any user in the system.

Fix: Uncomment the @token_required decorator and restore the current_user parameter to enforce authentication on this endpoint.

Unscoped Profile Query Allows Enumeration of Any User

The /api/v1/profile endpoint accepts any user_id parameter and returns the corresponding user's profile data without scoping the query to the authenticated user. Combined with the missing authentication, this allows complete enumeration of all user profiles including PII (email, country), roles, and permissions.

Impact: Attacker can enumerate all users and retrieve their PII, roles, and permissions by iterating user_id values.

Fix: Add authentication and ensure the query is scoped to the authenticated user's own profile, or require admin role for accessing other users' profiles.

Mass Assignment - Role Escalation via update_profile

The update_profile endpoint accepts 'role' from the form data and uses it to update the user's role in the database. Although the edit_profile.html template comments out the role field, an attacker can craft a POST request with a 'role' parameter to escalate their privileges (e.g., setting role to 'admin').

Impact: An attacker can escalate their privileges to admin or any other role by including a 'role' field in the POST request, gaining unauthorized access to administrative functionality.

Fix: Do not accept 'role' or 'permissions' from user-submitted form data. These fields should only be modifiable by administrators through a separate, authorized endpoint.

Unrestricted File Upload - No File Type Validation

The upload_file endpoint saves uploaded files with their original filename without any validation of file type, extension, or content. An attacker can upload executable files (e.g., .py, .sh, .html, .php) or files with malicious content.

Impact: An attacker could upload files with path traversal characters in the filename (e.g., '../../../etc/cron.d/malicious') to write files to arbitrary locations on the server, or upload malicious executable content.

Fix: Use werkzeug.utils.secure_filename() to sanitize the filename. Validate file extensions against an allowlist. Store files with generated names rather than user-supplied names.

Weak Password Hashing Using MD5

The custom_hash function uses MD5 (hashlib.md5) to hash passwords. MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password hashing — it is fast to compute, has no salt, and is vulnerable to rainbow table and brute-force attacks.

Impact: If the database is compromised, all user passwords can be quickly cracked using precomputed rainbow tables or brute force. This application stores PII, making this especially critical.

Fix: Use a proper password hashing algorithm like bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 with appropriate work factors and automatic salting.

Plaintext Password Storage via MD5 (No Proper KDF)

The users table schema stores passwords as VARCHAR(255) with no indication of proper key derivation function usage. Combined with the MD5 hashing in create_dummy_data.py, passwords are stored as unsalted MD5 hashes which are effectively equivalent to plaintext given modern cracking capabilities. The schema design does not enforce or facilitate proper password storage.

Impact: All user passwords stored in this table are protected only by MD5 hashing (as shown in the seed data script), making them trivially recoverable if the database is compromised.

Fix: Use a proper password hashing library (bcrypt, argon2) that produces salted hashes. The schema field size is adequate for bcrypt hashes (~60 chars), but the application code must use a proper KDF.

Hardcoded Credentials for User 'bob_brown'

User 'bob_brown' is seeded with the hardcoded password 'my-secret' and has 'write' permissions.

Impact: Anyone with knowledge of the source code can log in as this user with write permissions.

Fix: Do not seed databases with hardcoded weak credentials.

Missing Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint

The POST /api/v1/login endpoint has no rate limiting or account lockout mechanism. An attacker can make unlimited login attempts to brute-force user credentials.

Impact: An attacker can perform unlimited brute-force attacks against user accounts to guess passwords.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint (e.g., using Flask-Limiter) and add account lockout after a configurable number of failed attempts.

Weak password hashing using custom_hash (MD5-based)

The login endpoint compares passwords using custom_hash() which is imported from server.routes. Based on the application context, this is an MD5-based hash function. MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password hashing - it lacks salting and is extremely fast to brute-force with modern hardware.

Impact: If the password database is compromised, attackers can quickly crack all passwords using rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force attacks. MD5 can compute billions of hashes per second on modern hardware.

Fix: Replace custom_hash with a proper password hashing algorithm such as bcrypt, argon2, or scrypt that includes salting and configurable work factors.

Mass Assignment Vulnerability in Profile Update (role and permissions fields)

The update-profile endpoint checks if the current user's role is 'admin' before allowing updates to 'role' and 'permissions' fields (line 201). However, the admin check is based on the user's current role in the database, which means if an attacker can first set their role to 'admin' through another vulnerability, they gain full control. More critically, the update_model Swagger definition (lines 37-44) explicitly includes 'role' and 'permissions' fields, advertising these as updatable. The check at line 187-188 compares data['user_id'] with session['user_id'], but the user_id field in the request body could be omitted entirely, bypassing this check while still sending role/permissions in the payload. The admin role check at line 201 is the only protection, but this is a self-referential authorization check — if an admin account is compromised, privilege escalation to all accounts is possible.

Impact: If an attacker gains admin access (or if the role check can be bypassed), they can escalate privileges for any account by modifying role and permissions fields.

Fix: Remove 'role' and 'permissions' from the API model entirely. Implement a separate admin-only endpoint for privilege management with proper RBAC checks.

Mass Assignment - Sensitive Fields in Update Profile Model

The update-profile endpoint allows admin users to update 'role' and 'permissions' fields based on the current user's role. However, the role check uses the current user's own profile data from the database (user_profile['role'] == 'admin'). If an attacker who is an admin updates their own profile, they can set arbitrary role and permissions values. More critically, the Swagger documentation (update_model at lines 37-44) explicitly advertises 'role' and 'permissions' as updatable fields, guiding attackers to attempt mass assignment.

Impact: Admin users can set arbitrary permissions. The Swagger docs expose the sensitive fields, making it easy for attackers to discover and attempt privilege escalation.

Fix: Remove 'role' and 'permissions' from the update_model. Implement a separate admin-only endpoint for managing user roles and permissions with proper authorization checks.

Debug Mode Enabled in Production Configuration

DEBUG is set to True in the application configuration. When Flask runs in debug mode, it exposes the interactive Werkzeug debugger, which allows arbitrary code execution on the server. It also exposes detailed stack traces and internal application state.

Impact: An attacker can trigger an error and use the Werkzeug interactive debugger to execute arbitrary Python code on the server, leading to full system compromise. Detailed error messages also leak internal application structure.

Fix: Set DEBUG = False for production deployments. Use environment variables to control debug mode: DEBUG = os.environ.get('FLASK_DEBUG', 'False').lower() == 'true'

Session Fixation - No Session Regeneration on Signup

After successful registration, the signup handler sets session variables but does not regenerate the session ID, similar to the login handler.

Impact: An attacker can fixate a session ID before registration and then hijack the newly created authenticated session.

Fix: Regenerate the session after successful registration, similar to the login fix.

Sensitive Data (Full User Object) Passed to Profile Template

The profile route passes the full user database row (from SELECT * FROM users) to the template context. This row includes the password hash and potentially other sensitive fields. The full user object crosses a trust boundary into the rendered template.

Impact: The password hash and other sensitive internal fields are available in the template context. If any template rendering issue occurs or the template is modified, these values could be exposed to the user.

Fix: Select only the specific fields needed for the profile display, or create a sanitized dictionary excluding sensitive fields like password before passing to the template.

Missing CSRF Protection on State-Changing Endpoints

The `update_profile` POST endpoint (and other state-changing endpoints like upload, delete) do not implement CSRF token validation. The application uses session-based authentication with cookies, making it vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks.

Impact: An attacker can craft a malicious page that submits forms on behalf of an authenticated user, potentially changing their profile (including role and permissions), uploading files, or deleting files.

Fix: Implement CSRF protection using Flask-WTF or a similar library. Add CSRF tokens to all forms and validate them on the server side.

Missing Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint

The `/login` endpoint has no rate limiting, allowing unlimited login attempts. Combined with the weak MD5 password hashing, this makes brute-force attacks highly effective.

Impact: An attacker can perform unlimited brute-force password guessing attacks against any user account without being throttled or locked out.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint using a library like Flask-Limiter. Consider account lockout after a number of failed attempts.

Unrestricted File Upload - No Extension/Type Validation

The file upload handler accepts any file without validating the file extension or MIME type. The original filename from the user is used directly. An attacker could upload executable files (e.g., .php, .py, .html with JavaScript) or other malicious content.

Impact: An attacker can upload malicious files such as web shells, HTML files with XSS payloads, or other dangerous content to the server.

Fix: Validate file extensions against an allowlist of permitted types. Sanitize filenames using `werkzeug.utils.secure_filename()`. Consider storing files with generated names rather than user-supplied names.
Unauthenticated Access to Profile Pictures
Session Cookie Missing Secure and SameSite Flags
DOM-based XSS via Unsanitized Comment Input
Hardcoded credentials in dummy data script
Information disclosure via detailed error messages
Debug mode enabled when running application
Unauthenticated access to profile pictures endpoint allows enumeration
Missing security headers in HTTP responses
Clickjacking vulnerability due to missing X-Frame-Options header
No file size limits on upload endpoint

OpenGrep binary downloaded over HTTPS without integrity verification

The OpenGrep CLI binary is downloaded directly from a GitHub release URL without any checksum or signature verification. If the release is compromised (via a supply chain attack on the opengrep repository, DNS hijacking, or a compromised GitHub account), a malicious binary could be executed in the CI pipeline with full access to the repository contents and any available secrets.

Impact: An attacker who compromises the opengrep release could execute arbitrary code in the CI environment, potentially exfiltrating secrets, modifying source code, or injecting backdoors.

Fix: Add checksum verification after download: download the expected SHA256 hash and verify with 'sha256sum -c'. Alternatively, use a pinned GitHub Action or container image instead of downloading a raw binary.

Missing Rate Limiting on Delete User Endpoint

The DELETE /api/v1/delete-user/<user_id> endpoint has no rate limiting. An attacker can rapidly enumerate and delete all user accounts in the system.

Impact: An attacker can rapidly delete all user accounts by iterating through sequential user IDs.

Fix: Add rate limiting to this endpoint and require admin authentication.

Error Message Leaks Database Details on Signup

The signup error handler prints the full database exception to the server console/logs, which may contain sensitive database schema information, constraint details, or other internal details.

Impact: Database error details logged to stdout could be captured in log files and expose internal schema information to anyone with log access.

Fix: Use proper logging with appropriate log levels and avoid logging raw exception details in production.

Predictable Profile Picture Filenames (Sequential IDs)

Profile pictures are saved with simple sequential integer filenames (1.png, 2.png, etc.). Combined with the unauthenticated profile_pictures endpoint, this makes it trivial to enumerate all profile pictures.

Impact: Attackers can easily enumerate and access all profile pictures by iterating through sequential IDs.

Fix: Use UUIDs or cryptographically random filenames for profile pictures.
Overly broad SQL SELECT statement retrieving unnecessary password hash
Overly broad SQL SELECT statement retrieving unnecessary password hash
Overly broad SQL SELECT statement retrieving unnecessary password hash
Missing explicit session lifetime configuration
Sensitive information disclosure via console output
Profile picture enumeration via sequential numeric IDs
Missing cache control headers for sensitive pages

ZAP DAST scan uses -I flag to always pass regardless of findings

The ZAP baseline scan is run with the `-I` flag which causes ZAP to return exit code 0 even when alerts are found. This means the DAST scan will never fail the CI/CD pipeline regardless of vulnerabilities discovered.

Impact: Dynamic application security testing findings (XSS, SQLi, etc.) will not block the pipeline, allowing vulnerable code to be merged and deployed to production.

Fix: Remove the `-I` flag or use `-l WARN` to set a threshold that causes the scan to fail on medium or higher severity findings.
Predictable file storage location using hardcoded relative paths
